Ayahs

سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١)
Whatever there is in the heavens and [whatever there is on] the earth glorifies Allah and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢)
To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth: He gives life and brings death, and He has power over all things. (2)
هُوَ ٱلْأَوَّلُ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرُ وَٱلظَّـٰهِرُ وَٱلْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٣)
He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Hidden, and He has knowledge of all things. (3)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا يَلِجُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا يَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا وَمَا يَنزِلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا يَعْرُجُ فِيهَا ۖ وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٤)
It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days; then settled on the Throne. He knows whatever enters the earth and whatever emerges from it and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends to it, and He is with you wherever you may be, and Allah watches what you do. (4)
لَّهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٥)
To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned. (5)
يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٦)
He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He knows best what is in the breasts. (6)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to urge the believers and incite them toward spending in the way of Allah (الإنفاق في سبيل الله), as indicated by the repeated emphasis on this command throughout its verses: Believe in Allah and His Apostle, and spend out of that which He has made you successors (57:7); Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan (57:11); and Indeed the charitable men and charitable women, and those who lend Allah a good loan (57:18). It has termed their spending as "lending" (إقراضا) to Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه). Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the best of those sought, and He does not fail His promise; He has promised them that if they lend to Him, He will multiply it for them and grant them a generous and abundant reward.

The Surah also points out that this spending is an act of godwariness (التقوى) — the protective consciousness of Allah that shields one from His displeasure — and faith in the Messenger, and that it entails the forgiveness of sins, the granting of two portions of mercy, the necessity of light, and even joining the ranks of the truthful (الصديقين) and the witnesses (الشهداء) before Allah, the Glorified.

Interspersed within its verses are items of knowledge pertaining to the Origin (المبدأ) — the source of all existence, Allah — and the Return (المعاد) — the ultimate destination of all creation in the Hereafter — as well as a call to godwariness (التقوى), sincerity of faith, asceticism (الزهد), and exhortation.

The Surah is Medinan, as evidenced by the context of its verses, and some have claimed a consensus (إجماع) among exegetes on this matter.

The Surah opens by glorifying (التسبيح) and hallowing (التنزيه) Him, the Exalted, followed by several of His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى). This is because the purpose of the Surah—urging toward spending—might carry a faint suggestion of a perceived need or deficiency on His part. Thus, it is similar to all the Surahs that begin with glorification, namely the Surahs of al-Hashr, al-Saff, al-Jumu'ah, and al-Taghabun, which commence with "Glorifies" (سبح) or "Glorify" (يسبح).

His saying, the Exalted: Whatever is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (57:1) Glorification (التسبيح) is hallowing (التنزيه), which is the negation of anything that implies deficiency or need, or anything that does not befit the presence of His perfection, the Exalted. The word "Whatever" (ما) is a relative pronoun referring to everything in the heavens and the earth, encompassing both rational beings (العقلاء) such as angels, humans, and jinn (الثقلين), and non-rational beings such as inanimate objects. The evidence for this is the subsequent mention of His attributes related to rational beings, such as giving life and knowledge of what is within the breasts.

The meaning is: everything in the heavens and the earth—which constitutes the entire universe—hallows Allah, the Glorified.

What is meant by their glorification is the literal meaning of glorification, not the metaphorical sense, which would be the mere indication of every existent's being in the heavens and the earth that it has a Creator hallowed from every deficiency and described by every perfection. Nor is it the general metaphor, which is the indication of every existent toward His hallowing, either by the tongue of speech (لسان القال) as with rational beings, or by the tongue of state (لسان الحال) as with non-rational existents. This is due to His saying, the Exalted: and there is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification. (17:44). Here, He makes an exception by stating that "you do not understand their glorification." If their glorification meant the indication of their existence toward His existence—which is the establishment of proof for people through their existence—or if it meant their glorification and praise by the tongue of state, these are things that people do understand, and thus the exception would have no meaning.

Therefore, the glorification of whatever is in the heavens and the earth is a true glorification and an utterance of hallowing in the literal sense of the word, even if we do not understand it. The Exalted said: They will say, ‘Allah gave us speech, He who gives speech to all things.’ (41:21).

And His saying: and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise (وهو العزيز الحكيم) means the One whose side is impregnable (المنيع), who overcomes and is never overcome; the One whose action is precise (المتقن), such that nothing occurs to His action that could spoil it for Him, nor does the objection of any objector attach to it.

His saying, the Exalted: To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He gives life and brings death, and He has power over all things. (57:2) The discourse is structured to denote exclusivity (الحصر); thus, He alone is the Sovereign (المليك) in the heavens and the earth, decreeing whatever He wills because He is the Originator of everything. Whatever is in the heavens and the earth depends on Him for its existence and the effects of its existence; therefore, there is no decree except His, and no kingdom or authority except His.

His saying: He gives life and brings death (يحيي ويميت) is an allusion to the Names "The Giver of Life" (المحيي) and "The Bringer of Death" (المميت). The absolute usage of "gives life and brings death" implies their encompassment of every instance of giving life and bringing death, such as His creating the angels alive without prior death, His giving life to the fetus in its mother's womb, His giving life to the dead in the Resurrection, His creating inanimate matter dead without prior life, His bringing death to man in this world, and His bringing death a second time in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — as indicated by His saying: ‘Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life.’ (40:11). The form "gives life and brings death" (يحيي ويميت) indicates continuity (الاستمرار).

His saying: and He has power over all things (وهو على كل شيء قدير) contains an allusion to the attribute of His power (القدرة) and that it is absolute, not restricted to one thing over another. The conclusion of the verse with the mention of power over all things is appropriate to the preceding mention of giving life and bringing death, as someone might imagine that there is no power to revive the dead once no trace or essence of them remains.

His saying, the Exalted: He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Hidden, and He has knowledge of all things. (57:3) Since He, the Exalted, is powerful over every conceivable thing, He encompasses everything with His power from every direction. Thus, whatever is assumed to be "first," He is before it; so He is the First (الأول) rather than the thing assumed to be first. Whatever is assumed to be "last," He is after it due to His power encompassing it from every side; so He is the Last (الآخر) rather than the thing assumed to be last. Whatever thing is assumed to be "manifest," He is more manifest than it because His power encompasses it from above it; so He is the Manifest (الظاهر) rather than the thing assumed to be manifest. And whatever thing is assumed to be "hidden," He, the Exalted, is more hidden than it because of His encompassment of it from behind it; so He is the Hidden (الباطن) rather than the thing assumed to be hidden. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the First, the Last, the Manifest, and the Hidden in an absolute sense, while these attributes in anything other than Him are relative and proportional.

His being the First, the Last, the Manifest, and the Hidden is neither temporal (زمانية) nor spatial (مكانية) in the sense of Him being contained by them; otherwise, He would not precede them nor be hallowed from them. Rather, He encompasses all things in whatever manner they are assumed and however they are conceived.

It becomes clear from the preceding that these four names—the First, the Last, the Manifest, and the Hidden—are branches of His name "The Encompassing" (المحيط), which is itself a branch of the absoluteness of His power (القدرة); for His power encompasses everything. It is also possible to derive these four names from the encompassment of His existence (الوجود) over everything; for He, the Exalted, is established before the establishment of everything, and established after the annihilation of everything, and is closer than every manifest thing, and more hidden from delusions and intellects than every concealed, hidden thing.

Similarly, these four names have a type of derivation from His knowledge (العلم), which is suited by the conclusion of the verse with His saying: and He has knowledge of all things (وهو بكل شيء عليم).

Some have interpreted the four names as: He is the First before everything, the Last after the destruction of everything, the Manifest through the proofs indicating Him, and the Hidden who is not perceived by the senses.

It has also been said: The First without a beginning, the Last without an end, the Manifest who is dominant and exalted over everything such that everything is beneath Him, and the Hidden who knows everything such that no one is more knowledgeable than Him.

It has also been said: The First without beginning, the Last without end, the Manifest without proximity, and the Hidden without concealment.

There are other opinions regarding their meanings that are not sound, which we have refrained from mentioning.

His saying, the Exalted: It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days (57:4). Its explanation has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: then He settled on the Throne. He knows whatever travels into the earth and whatever comes out of it, and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends into it. (57:4). A detailed discussion on the meaning of the Throne (العرش) preceded in Surah al-A'raf, verse 54.

It was previously mentioned that "settling on the Throne" is a metaphor for taking up the governance of the kingdom (تدبير الملك); therefore, He followed it with the mention of knowledge of particular states, because knowledge is a necessary concomitant of governance.

His saying: He knows whatever travels into the earth and whatever comes out of it, and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends into it. Traveling into (الولوج)—as al-Raghib said—is entering into a narrow space, and ascending (العروج) is going upward. The meaning is: He knows what enters and penetrates the earth, such as rainwater, seeds, and other things; what comes out of the earth, such as types of plants, animals, and water; what descends from the sky, such as rain, rays, and angels; and what ascends and goes up into it, such as vapors, angels, and the deeds of the servants.

His saying, the Exalted: and He is with you wherever you may be (57:4) because of His encompassment of you; thus, you are never absent from Him wherever you are, in whatever time you live, or in whatever state you are assumed to be. He mentioned the generality of places—"wherever you may be"—because the most familiar way for one thing to separate from another and be absent from it is by changing location; otherwise, His relation to all places, times, and states is the same.

It has been said: "Withness" (المعية) is a metaphor for His encompassment in knowledge (الإحاطة العلمية).

His saying, the Exalted: and Allah sees best what you do. (57:4). This is like a branch resulting from what preceded it—His being with them wherever they are and His being knowledgeable of all things. For the necessary consequence of His presence with them without any separation or concealment, while He is All-knowing, is that He is All-seeing (بصيرا) of their actions, seeing the outward aspect of their deeds and what is in their inward selves of intention and purpose.

His saying, the Exalted: To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned. (57:5). He repeated His saying: "To Him belongs the kingdom..." etc., because the return of all things to Him is built upon the generality of His kingdom; thus, He stated it explicitly to indicate this foundation. The Exalted said: The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’ (40:16).

His saying: and to Allah all matters are returned (وإلى الله ترجع الأمور). "Matters" (الأمور) is a plural defined by the definite article, which denotes generality, as in His saying: Look! To Allah do all matters eventually come. (42:53). Thus, there is nothing but that it returns to Allah, and there is no one to return them to Allah except Him, because the kingdom belongs exclusively to Him; so to Him belongs the command and to Him belongs the judgment.

In the verse, the manifest noun "Allah" is placed where a pronoun would be in "to Allah" (إلى الله), and likewise in the previous verse "and Allah sees best what you do." Perhaps the reason for this is so that the two sentences may strike their hearts like a well-known proverb, because of the upcoming mention of the Day of Resurrection and the magnificent reward for those who spend in the way of Allah therein.

His saying, the Exalted: He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He knows best what is in the breasts. (57:6). Making the night pass into the day and the day into the night refers to the variation of the night and day in length and shortness according to the seasons of the year in each of the northern and southern regions, each being the opposite of the other. This has been mentioned in His speech, the Exalted, more than once.

What is meant by "what is in the breasts" (ذات الصدور) are the concealed thoughts and hidden intentions that accompany the breasts, because they are attributed to the hearts, and the hearts are in the breasts. The sentence—meaning His saying: and He knows best what is in the breasts—is an explanation of His knowledge encompassing what is in the breasts, after explaining His sight encompassing the outward aspects of their deeds with His saying: and Allah sees best what you do.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by Ahmad, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it as fair), al-Nasa'i, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman, from 'Irbad ibn Sariyah: that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to recite the Musabbihat (the Surahs beginning with glorification) before he went to sleep, and he said: "In them is a verse better than a thousand verses."

I say: It is also narrated from Ibn al-Durays from Yahya ibn Abi Kathir from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from 'Asim ibn Humayd, he said: Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام was asked about monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and he said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, knew that in the end of times there would be people who delve deeply [into matters], so Allah the Exalted revealed: Say, ‘He is Allah, the One’ (Surah 112) and the verses from Surah al-Hadid up to His saying: and He knows best what is in the breasts. So whoever seeks beyond that has perished."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding Whatever is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise, he said: It is His saying: "I have been given the comprehensive words (جوامع الكلم)." And regarding His saying: He is the First, he said: "Meaning before everything." and the Last, he said: "He remains after everything." and He knows best what is in the breasts, he said: "Of the secret thoughts (الضمائر)."

In al-Kafi, it is narrated: that he—meaning Ali عليه‌السلام—was asked: "Where was our Lord before He created the heaven and the earth?" He replied: "'Where' is a question about place (مكان), and Allah was while there was no place."

In al-Tawhid, a sermon by al-Hasan ibn Ali عليه‌السلام contains: "Praise be to Allah, for whom there was no known first (أول معلوم), nor a finite last (آخر متناه), nor a perceivable 'before' (قبل مدرك), nor a limited 'after' (بعد محدود). Thus, intellects and their delusions, and thought and its passing reflections, and minds and their perceptions do not grasp His attribute, such that one could say: 'When?' or 'Begun from what?' or 'Manifest upon what?' or 'Hidden within what?'"

I say: His saying "known first" etc., are explanatory descriptions; meaning He has no first, for if He had a first, it would be possible for knowledge to attach to it. Nor a last, for if He had a last, He would be finite. Nor a "before," for if there were, it would be possible to perceive it. Nor an "after," otherwise He would be limited.

His saying: "Begun from what?" means He did not begin from anything such that He would have a first. "Manifest upon what?" means He does not excel over something by occurring and settling upon it, like a body upon a body. "Hidden within what?" means He is not hidden within something by entering it and being concealed by it.

In Nahj al-Balagha: "Every manifest thing other than Him is not hidden, and every hidden thing other than Him is not manifest."

I say: Its meaning is that the aspect of manifestation (الظهور) in anything other than Him, the Exalted, is different from the aspect of hiddenness (البطون), and vice versa. As for Him, the Exalted, since He is unitary in essence (أحدي الذات), neither dividing nor partitioning into one side and another, He is manifest from the very aspect He is hidden, and hidden from the very aspect He is manifest. Thus, He is a hidden one, concealed due to the perfection of His manifestation, and a manifest one, clear due to the perfection of His hiddenness.

Also in it: "Praise be to Allah, the First, so there is nothing before Him; and the Last, so there is nothing after Him; and the Manifest, so there is nothing above Him; and the Hidden, so there is nothing beneath Him."

I say: What is meant by priority (القبلية) and posteriority (البعدية) is not temporal priority and posteriority, such that one would assume a temporal extension without ends, in which the world occupies a portion while its two ends are empty of it, and the existence of Allah, the Hallowed and Exalted, would correspond to the entirety of time, with nothing of its two sides being empty of Him even if they went to infinity—such that His existence, the Exalted, would precede the world in time and follow it in time. If it were so, He, the Exalted, would be subject to change in His essence and states by the change of times renewing upon Him, and His priority and posteriority would be subordinate to time, and time would be the "First" and the "Last" essentially.

Likewise, His manifestation and hiddenness are not according to place (المكان) in a similar vein. Rather, He, the Exalted, precedes by His very transcendent essence every assumed thing, and is the last by His very essence after every matter assumed to be last; and He is manifest and hidden likewise. Time is His creation, posterior to Him.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu al-Shaykh narrates in al-'Azamah from Ibn Umar and Abu Sa'id from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "People will not cease asking about everything until they say: 'This is Allah, He was before everything, so what was before Allah?' If they say that to you, then say: 'He is the First before everything, and He is the Last so there is nothing after Him, and He is the Manifest above everything, and He is the Hidden beneath everything, and He has knowledge of all things.'"

In al-Tawhid, with its chain to Abu Basir, he said: I heard Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام saying: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, our Lord, has always been, and knowledge (العلم) was His essence while there was nothing to be known (معلوم). Then, when He brought things into existence, the knowledge from Him fell upon the known."

I say: What is meant by this knowledge is not mental images (الصور الذهنية), such that He, the Exalted, would be like the builder of a house who conceives a form and shape for the house before building it, then builds it according to what he conceived, so the mental image corresponds to the external structure, and then the house is demolished while the mental image remains as it was. This is what is called "universal knowledge" (العلم الكلي), and it is impossible for Him, the Exalted. Rather, His essence, the Exalted, is identical to the knowledge of His object of knowledge; then, when the object of knowledge is realized in the external world, the essence of the object of knowledge is identical to His knowledge of it. The first is called "Essential Knowledge" (العلم الذاتي) and the second "Active Knowledge" (العلم الفعلي).

Also in it, a sermon by Ali عليه‌السلام contains: "And He knew them not by an instrument (أداة) without which knowledge cannot be; and there is not between Him and His object of knowledge any knowledge other than Him."

I say: What is meant by this is that His essence, the Exalted, is identical to His knowledge, and there is no additional form (صورة زائدة).

Ayahs

ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَأَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا جَعَلَكُم مُّسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (٧)
Have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and spend out of that of which He has made you heirs. There is a great reward for those of you who have faith and spend [in Allah’s way]. (7)
وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ وَٱلرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِرَبِّكُمْ وَقَدْ أَخَذَ مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٨)
Why should you not have faith in Allah when the Apostle invites you to have faith in your Lord, and He has certainly made a covenant with you, if you are [genuinely] faithful? (8)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ لِّيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩)
It is He who sends down manifest signs to His servant that He may bring you out of darkness into light, and indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to you. (9)
وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَا يَسْتَوِى مِنكُم مَّنْ أَنفَقَ مِن قَبْلِ ٱلْفَتْحِ وَقَـٰتَلَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنفَقُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَقَـٰتَلُوا۟ ۚ وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١٠)
Why should you not spend in the way of Allah, when to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth? Those of you who spent [their means] and fought before the victory are not equal [to others]. They are greater in rank than those who have spent and fought afterwards. Yet Allah has promised the best reward to each and Allah is well aware of what you do. (10)
مَّن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يُقْرِضُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا فَيُضَـٰعِفَهُۥ لَهُۥ وَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ (١١)
Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan, that He may multiply it for him and [that] there may be a noble reward for him? (11)
يَوْمَ تَرَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ يَسْعَىٰ نُورُهُم بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِم بُشْرَىٰكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٢)
The day you will see the faithful, men and women, with their light moving swiftly in front of them and on their right, [and they are greeted with the words:] ‘There is good news for you today! Gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great success.’ (12)
يَوْمَ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتُ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱنظُرُونَا نَقْتَبِسْ مِن نُّورِكُمْ قِيلَ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ وَرَآءَكُمْ فَٱلْتَمِسُوا۟ نُورًا فَضُرِبَ بَيْنَهُم بِسُورٍ لَّهُۥ بَابٌۢ بَاطِنُهُۥ فِيهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةُ وَظَـٰهِرُهُۥ مِن قِبَلِهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ (١٣)
The day the hypocrites, men and women, will say to the faithful, ‘Please wait, that we may glean something from your light!’ They will be told: ‘Go back and grope for light!’ Then there will be set up between them a wall with a gate, with mercy within and punishment without. (13)
يُنَادُونَهُمْ أَلَمْ نَكُن مَّعَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنتُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ وَٱرْتَبْتُمْ وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْأَمَانِىُّ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَغَرَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ (١٤)
They will call out to them, ‘Did we not use to be with you?’ They will say, ‘Yes! But you cast yourselves into perdition. You awaited and were skeptical, and [false] hopes deceived you until the edict of Allah came, and the Deceiver deceived you concerning Allah. (14)
فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ وَلَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ مَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ هِىَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٥)
So today no ransom shall be taken from you or the faithless. The Fire will be your abode: it is your [ultimate] refuge and an evil destination.’ (15)

(Commentary)

This is an emphatic command to spend in the way of Allah, specifically regarding struggle (الجهاد) — the exertion of effort in the cause of Allah — as supported by His saying: لَا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَقَاتَلَ (Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought) [Qarai 57:10]. This is further supported by the view that His saying: آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَأَنْفِقُوا (Believe in Allah and His Apostle, and spend...) [Qarai 57:7] was revealed during the Expedition of Tabuk.

His saying, the Exalted: آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَأَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا جَعَلَكُمْ مُسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ (Believe in Allah and His Apostle, and spend out of that in which He has made you successors...) [Qarai 57:7]. What is understood from the context of the verses is that the address in this verse is to the believers in Allah and His Messenger, not to the disbelievers, nor to the believers and disbelievers together as has been suggested. The command to those who have already embraced faith (الإيمان) in Allah and His Messenger to "believe" means a command to realize the reality of faith by manifesting its practical effects. For if a quality such as generosity, chastity, or courage were truly established in a person's soul, its specific effect would never fail to manifest. Among the effects of faith in Allah and His Messenger is obedience to what Allah and His Messenger have commanded.

From here, two points become clear: First, the command to a believer to believe is, in reality, a command to one who has attained a certain level of faith to ascend to a higher level. This type of command contains an allusion (إيماء) that what the commanded person currently possesses of the commanded matter does not fully satisfy the Commander.

Second, His saying: آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَأَنْفِقُوا (Believe in Allah and His Apostle, and spend) is a command to spend while hinting that it is an effect of a quality they already possess; thus, they ought to spend because they are characterized by it. This effectively makes their faith the underlying cause (تعليل) for the spending.

Regarding His saying: وَأَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا جَعَلَكُمْ مُسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ (and spend out of that in which He has made you successors), the "succession" (استخلاف) of man means making him a successor (خليفة). This refers either to their succession from Allah, the Glorified, as they succeed Him on earth, as indicated by His saying: إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً (Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth) [Qarai 2:30]. Describing the wealth in their hands with this expression serves to clarify the reality and to encourage them to spend. For if they are certain that the wealth belongs to Allah and that they are merely successors over it—deputies (وكلاء) on His behalf who dispose of it as He has permitted—it becomes easy for them to spend it, and their souls do not feel constrained by doing so.

Alternatively, it refers to their succession of the generations that preceded them, just as every generation succeeds the one before it. This expression also encourages spending; for if they remember that this wealth belonged to others and did not remain with them, they will realize that it likewise will not remain with them and that they will leave it for others. Thus, spending it becomes easy for them, and their souls become generous with it.

His saying: فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَأَنْفَقُوا لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (So those of you who believe and spend shall have a great reward) [Qarai 57:7] is a promise of reward for spending to reinforce the encouragement. What is meant by faith here is faith in Allah and His Messenger.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَالرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِرَبِّكُمْ (Why should you not believe in Allah while the Apostle invites you to believe in your Lord...) [Qarai 57:8]. What is meant by faith is faith such that its effects follow from it—among which is spending in the way of Allah. Or if you prefer, it means manifesting the effects of the faith they already possess.

His saying: وَالرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِرَبِّكُمْ (while the Apostle invites you to believe in your Lord). He expressed the Lord as "the Lord" (الرب) and annexed it to them as an allusion to the reason for the invitation and the command. It is as if it were said: He invites you to believe in Allah because He is your Lord, and it is obligatory upon you to believe in Him.

His saying: وَقَدْ أَخَذَ مِيثَاقَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ (and He has already taken a pledge from you, if you are [truly] believers) [Qarai 57:8] is a reinforcement of the rebuke understood from the beginning of the verse. The pronoun in "He has taken" (أخذ) refers either to Allah, the Glorified, or to the Messenger. In either case, the "pledge" (الميثاق) — the solemn covenant of loyalty and obedience — refers to what is indicated by their testimony to the oneness of Allah and the messengership of His Messenger on the day they believed in him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), signifying that they are committed to hearing and obeying.

It has been said that the pledge refers to the covenant taken from them in the realm of pre-existence (الذر). According to this, the pronoun in "He has taken" refers to Allah, the Glorified. However, this is far-fetched given the context of the argument against them, as they are heedless of it. Furthermore, the taking of the pledge in the pre-existent realm is not exclusive to the believers but encompasses the hypocrites and the disbelievers as well.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُنَزِّلُ عَلى عَبْدِهِ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لِيُخْرِجَكُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ (It is He who sends down manifest signs to His servant that He may bring you out of darkness into light...) [Qarai 57:9]. The "manifest signs" (الآيات البينات) refer to the verses of the Noble Quran which clarify for them the religious obligations (فرائض) incumbent upon them. The subject of "that He may bring you out" (ليخرجكم) is the pronoun referring to Allah or to His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The ultimate reference of the second is also the first; for the pledge is His pledge, and He took it through the medium of His Messenger or without a medium, just as faith in Him and His Messenger is faith in Him. Therefore, He said at the beginning of the verse: وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ (Why should you not believe in Allah), mentioning Himself and not mentioning His Messenger, to indicate that faith in His Messenger is faith in Him.

His saying: وَإِنَّ اللهَ بِكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ (and indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to you) [Qarai 57:9]. Ending the verse with His kindness and mercy is an indication that the faith to which His Messenger invites them is for their own good and benefit; they are the ones who profit from it, not Allah or His Messenger. Thus, it contains a reinforcement of the encouragement toward faith and spending.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنْفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلِلَّهِ مِيراثُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (Why should you not spend in the way of Allah, while to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth?...) [Qarai 57:10]. Heritage (الميراث) and legacy (التراث) refer to wealth that passes from the deceased to those who remain after him among his heirs. The annexation of "heritage" to "the heavens and the earth" is explanatory (بيانية); thus, the heavens and the earth are themselves the heritage, including all the things created from them that are possessed by the sentient beings (ذوو الشعور) inhabiting them. The heavens and the earth encompass everything within them that was created from them.

Sentient beings, such as humans, for example, dispose of these things by designating what they use for themselves, which is the conventional ownership (الملك الاعتباري) that Allah, the Glorified, guided them to conceptualize among themselves so that the aspects of their worldly life might be organized.

However, they do not remain, nor does what they possess remain for them; rather, the death decreed among them takes them away, and what was in their hands passes to those after them, and so on, until everyone perishes and none remains but He, the Glorified.

The earth, for instance, and what is in it and upon it of wealth, is a heritage in the sense that every generation of its inhabitants inherits it from the one before it; thus, it is a constant heritage circulating among them, successor after predecessor. It is also a heritage in the sense that they will all perish, and nothing will remain of it except for Allah, who made them successors over it.

To Allah, the Glorified, belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth in both senses. As for the first: because He is the one who grants them ownership of the wealth, and He is the true Owner of what He has given them to possess. The Exalted said: لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth) [Qarai 31:26], and He said: وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth) [Qarai 24:42], and He said: وَآتُوهُمْ مِنْ مَالِ اللَّهِ الَّذِي آتَاكُمْ (and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you) [Qarai 24:33].

As for the second sense: it is evident from the verses concerning the Resurrection, such as His saying: كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ (Everyone on it is ephemeral) [Qarai 55:26] and others. What first comes to mind is that the meaning of them being a "heritage" is this second sense.

In any case, the verse contains a severe rebuke for their failure to spend in the way of Allah from the wealth that, in reality, none shall inherit but He, the Exalted, and which shall remain neither for them nor for anyone else. The use of the explicit noun in place of a pronoun in His saying: وَلِلَّهِ (and to Allah) is to intensify the rebuke.

His saying, the Exalted: لَا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَقَاتَلَ أُولئِكَ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَنْفَقُوا مِنْ بَعْدُ وَقَاتَلُوا (Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought. They are higher in rank than those who spent afterwards and fought...) [Qarai 57:10]. Equality (الاستواء) means being the same. The counterpart to His saying: "those who spent before the conquest and fought" is omitted for the sake of conciseness (إيجاز), as it is indicated by His saying: "They are higher in rank than those who spent afterwards and fought."

The "conquest" (الفتح) refers—as has been said—to the Conquest of Mecca or the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. The coupling of fighting (القتال) with spending (الإنفاق) is not devoid of an allusion, or rather a demonstration, that the spending in the way of Allah encouraged in these verses is spending for the sake of struggle (الجهاد).

It is as if the verse is intended to clarify that spending in the way of Allah, the more it is hastened toward, the more beloved it is to Allah and the greater its rank and station. Otherwise, it is evident that these verses were revealed after the conquest and the fighting that they rushed toward before the conquest, and after some of the battles that occurred after it.

His saying: وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللهُ الْحُسْنى (and to each Allah has promised the best reward) [Qarai 57:10]; that is, Allah has promised the best reward to everyone who spent and fought before the conquest, and everyone who spent and fought after it, even though the first group is greater in rank than the second. In this, there is a consolation for the hearts of those who were delayed in spending and fighting, that they have a share of His mercy and are not absolutely deprived; thus, there is no reason for them to despair of it even if they were delayed.

His saying: وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (and Allah is well aware of what you do) [Qarai 57:10] is a concluding remark (تذييل) related to all that preceded. It contains an intensification of the rebuke and a confirmation and establishment of His saying: "Not equal among you..." and His saying: "and to each Allah has promised the best reward." It is possible that it relates only to the last sentence, but its relation to the whole is more general and comprehensive.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يُقْرِضُ اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً فَيُضاعِفَهُ لَهُ وَلَهُ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ (Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan, that He may multiply it for him, and [that] there may be a noble reward for him?) [Qarai 57:11]. Al-Raghib said: "That which is given to a person of wealth on the condition of returning its equivalent is called a loan (قرض)." [End quote]. It is said in al-Majma': "Its root is 'cutting' (القطع); thus, it is a portion cut off from its owner with his permission on the guarantee of returning its like." He said: "Multiplication (المضاعفة) is an increase over the original amount by its like or many times its like." [End quote]. Al-Raghib said: "Reward (الأجر) and wages (الأجرة) refer to what returns as a recompense for an action, whether worldly or otherworldly." He said: "It is only used for benefit, not for harm, unlike 'recompense' (الجزاء), which is used for both benefit and harm." [End quote summary].

What the Exalted gives of reward for the servant's deed is a favor (تفضل) from Him, without any entitlement on the part of the servant. For the servant and the deeds he performs are the absolute property of Him, the Glorified—an ownership that does not accept transfer or transition. However, He has considered, by way of a legislative consideration (اعتبار تشريعي), the servant as an owner and his deed as his property—while He is the true Owner of what He has given him to possess, which is another favor. Then He chose what He loved of his deeds and promised him a reward for his deed, calling it "wages" (أجر) and "recompense" (جزاء), which is yet another favor. None benefits from this in this world or the hereafter except the servant. The Exalted said: لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا مِنْهُمْ وَاتَّقَوْا أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (For those of them who are virtuous and God-conscious there shall be a great reward) [Qarai 3:172], and He said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ (As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there is for them an undiminished reward) [Qarai 41:8], and He said after describing Paradise and its bliss: إِنَّ هَذَا كَانَ لَكُمْ جَزَاءً وَكَانَ سَعْيُكُمْ مَشْكُورًا (This is indeed your reward, and your endeavor has been appreciated) [Qarai 76:22]. What He promised of appreciation (الشكر) and the absence of obligation (عدم المن) when granting the reward is the completion of the favor.

In the verse, there is an eloquent urging toward the encouraged spending in the way of Allah, as He asks who among them will spend in the way of Allah, likening his spending to a loan he gives to Allah, the Glorified, which He must return. Then He definitively states that He will not return its mere equivalent to him, but will multiply it. He did not stop there but added to it a "noble reward" (أجر كريم) in the hereafter. A noble reward is that which is pleasing in its kind, and the otherworldly reward is such because it is the ultimate conceivable blessing at the time of the ultimate conceivable need.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَرَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ يَسْعى نُورُهم بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ (The day you will see the faithful men and the faithful women, with their light moving swiftly before them and on their right...) [Qarai 57:12]. "The day" (اليوم) is the adverbial qualifier for His saying: "there may be a noble reward for him," and it refers to the Day of Resurrection. The address in "you will see" (ترى) is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or to every listener capable of being addressed. It appears that the preposition "with" (بـ) in "on their right" (بأيمانهم) means "in."

The meaning is: For the one who lends Allah a good loan, there is a noble reward on the day when you, O Messenger—or anyone capable of seeing—will see the men and women who believe in Allah and His Messenger, their light (نور) — the spiritual radiance resulting from faith and righteous deeds — moving swiftly in front of them and in their right hands. The "right" (اليمين) is the direction from which their felicity (سعادة) comes.

The verse is general, encompassing the believers of all nations and is not restricted to this nation. Expressing the radiance of the light as "moving swiftly" (سعي) suggests that they are hastening toward the degrees of Paradise that Allah, the Glorified, has prepared for them. The directions of felicity and the stations of proximity (مقامات القرب) are illuminated for them one after another until their light is completed for them, as the Exalted said: وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ زُمَرًا (Those who were God-conscious of their Lord will be led to Paradise in throngs) [Qarai 39:73], and He said: يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمَنِ وَفْدًا (The day We shall muster the God-conscious toward the All-beneficent, like guests of honor) [Qarai 19:85], and He said: يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِي اللَّهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَى بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا (the day Allah will not let the Prophet down and those who have faith with him. Their light will move swiftly before them and on their right, while they say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us...’) [Qarai 66:8].

The commentators have various opinions regarding the interpretation of the individual words of the verse, which we have overlooked due to the lack of evidence from the wording of the verse itself. What is found in the transmitted narrations will be presented in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," if Allah wills.

His saying: بُشْراكُمُ الْيَوْمَ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها (‘Good news for you today! Gardens with conduits running amidst them, to remain in them [forever]...’) [Qarai 57:12] is a narration of what will be said to the believing men and women on the Day of Resurrection. The speakers are the angels by the command of Allah, and the implied meaning is: It will be said to them: "Your good news..." etc. What is meant by "good news" (البشرى) is that which is announced, which is Paradise, and the rest is clear.

His saying: ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ (...That is the great success) [Qarai 57:12] is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, and the reference is to the aforementioned moving of the light and the good news. Or it is part of the completion of the angels' speech, and the reference is to the gardens and the eternal dwelling therein.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالْمُنافِقاتُ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا انْظُرُونا نَقْتَبِسْ مِنْ نُورِكُمْ (The day the hypocrites, men and women, will say to the faithful, ‘Wait for us, so that we may take a light from your light!’...) [Qarai 57:13]. The verb "to look" (النظر), when it is transitive by itself, conveys the meaning of waiting and granting respite. When it is transitive with "toward" (إلى), it means casting one's sight toward something. When it is transitive with "in" (في), it means reflection. "Acquiring" (الاقتباس) is taking a flame (قبس) from a fire.

The context indicates that they are on that day in a darkness whose canopy has surrounded them, and they are forced to travel toward their eternal abode. However, the believing men and women travel by their light which moves swiftly before them and on their right; thus, they see the path and are guided to their stations. As for the hypocrites (المنافقون) — those who outwardly profess faith while inwardly concealing disbelief — they are covered in darkness, unable to find a way. They are with the believers just as they were with them in the world, counted among them. Then the believing men and women outpace them toward Paradise, while the hypocrites, men and women, fall behind in a darkness that covers them. They ask the believing men and women to wait for them so they may catch up and take a flame from their light to illuminate their way.

His saying: قِيلَ ارْجِعُوا وَراءَكُمْ فَالْتَمِسُوا نُوراً (It will be said, ‘Go back and look for a light!’...) [Qarai 57:13]. The speaker is either the angels or a group of the perfected believers, such as the People of the Elevations (أصحاب الأعراف).

In any case, it is from Allah and by His permission. The address: "Go back and look for a light!" has been said to be an address based on mockery and derision, just as they used to mock the believers in the world. More apparent, based on this, is that "back" (الوراء) refers to the world. The summary of the meaning is: Return to the world which you left behind your backs and in which you performed what you performed of hypocrisy, and seek light from those deeds; for light is only the light of deeds or faith, and you have neither faith nor deeds.

This can be considered a distinct interpretation without the meaning of mockery, such that His saying: "Go back" is a command to return to the world and acquire light through faith and righteous deeds, though they are not returning and are unable to do so. Thus, the command to return is like the command to prostrate mentioned in His saying: يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ وَقَدْ كانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سالِمُونَ (The day when the shank is bared, and they are summoned to prostrate themselves, but they will not be able [to do it]. Their looks will be cast down, and abasement will overcome them, for they were summoned to prostrate themselves while they were yet whole) [Qarai 68:42-43].

It has also been said: The meaning is, return to the place where the light was distributed and seek it from there. So they return but find nothing, and they turn back toward the believers, only to find that a wall has been set up between them. This is a deception from Him, the Exalted, by which He deceives them just as they used to deceive Him in the world, as He said: إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ يُخادِعُونَ اللهَ وَهُوَ خادِعُهُمْ (The hypocrites indeed seek to deceive Allah, but it is He who outwits them...) [Qarai 4:142].

His saying, the Exalted: فَضُرِبَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِسُورٍ لَهُ بابٌ باطِنُهُ فِيهِ الرَّحْمَةُ وَظاهِرُهُ مِنْ قِبَلِهِ الْعَذابُ (...Then a wall will be set up between them, with a door in it: its inside contains mercy and its outside is toward punishment) [Qarai 57:13]. The "wall" (السور) of a city is its barrier that separates its interior from its exterior. The pronoun in "a wall will be set up between them" refers to both the believers and the hypocrites; that is, a barrier wall will be set up between the believers and the hypocrites, separating one group from the other.

It has been said: The wall is the Elevations (الأعراف), which is not far-fetched, and an indication of this was previously mentioned in the interpretation of His saying: وَبَيْنَهُما حِجابٌ وَعَلَى الْأَعْرافِ رِجالٌ (And there will be a veil between them. And on the Elevations will be certain men...) [Qarai 7:46]. It has also been said that the wall is other than the Elevations.

His saying: لَهُ بابٌ (with a door in it); that is, the wall has a door. This resembles the state of the hypocrites in the world, for they were among the believers, having connection and association with them, yet they were nonetheless veiled from them by a barrier. Furthermore, they see the people of Paradise, which increases their regret and remorse.

His saying: باطِنُهُ فِيهِ الرَّحْمَةُ وَظاهِرُهُ مِنْ قِبَلِهِ الْعَذابُ (its inside contains mercy and its outside is toward punishment). "Its inside" (باطنه) is a subject, and the phrase "contains mercy" (فيه الرحمة) is a subject and predicate forming the news (خبر) for "its inside." Likewise, "its outside" (ظاهره) is a subject, and the phrase "is toward punishment" (من قبله العذاب) is a subject and predicate forming its news. The pronouns in "contains" (فيه) and "toward" (من قبله) refer to the inside and the outside.

It appears from the fact that the inside of the wall contains mercy and its outside is toward punishment that the wall surrounds the believers, who are within it, while the hypocrites are outside of it.

In the fact that its interior, which faces the believers, contains mercy, and its exterior, which faces the hypocrites, contains punishment, there is a correspondence to the state of faith in the world. For faith is a blessing for the people of sincerity among the believers, in which they rejoice and find pleasure, while it is a punishment for the people of hypocrisy, who feel constrained by professing it and suffer because of it.

His saying, the Exalted: يُنادُونَهُمْ أَلَمْ نَكُنْ مَعَكُمْ (They will call out to them, ‘Were we not with you?’...) [Qarai 57:14]. This is a new sentence (استئناف) serving as an answer to an implied question, as if it were asked: "What will the hypocrites, men and women, do after the wall is set up and they witness the punishment from its exterior?" So it was said: "They will call out to them," etc.

The meaning is: The hypocrites, men and women, will call out to the believing men and women, saying: "Were we not with you?" meaning their being with the believing men and women in the world in the outward appearance of the religion.

His saying: قالُوا بَلى (They will say, ‘Yes!’...) [Qarai 57:14] is the answer of the believing men and women to them. The meaning is: "They will say," i.e., the believing men and women will say in response to them, "Yes, you were with us in the world, وَلَكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنْتُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ (but you tempted yourselves) [Qarai 57:14]; that is, you tried and destroyed yourselves, وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ (and you awaited [misfortune for us]) [Qarai 57:14] regarding the religion and its people, وَارْتَبْتُمْ (and you were in doubt) [Qarai 57:14] concerning your religion, وَغَرَّتْكُمُ الْأَمانِيُّ (and [false] hopes deluded you) [Qarai 57:14]—among which was your hope that the light of the religion would be extinguished and its people would abandon it—حَتَّى جاءَ أَمْرُ اللهِ (until Allah’s edict arrived) [Qarai 57:14], which is death, وَغَرَّكُمْ بِاللهِ الْغَرُورُ (and the Deceiver deceived you concerning Allah) [Qarai 57:14]. The "Deceiver" (الغرور) with a fatha on the ghayn is Satan.

The verse—as you see—indicates that the hypocrites, men and women, seek help from the believing men and women against the darkness they are in, pleading that they were with them in the world. Then it indicates that the believing men and women answer that they were indeed with them, but their hearts did not correspond to their outward state, as they tempted themselves, awaited misfortune, doubted, were deluded by false hopes, and were deceived concerning Allah by the Deceiver. These wicked qualities are maladies of the heart; thus, their hearts were not sound (سليم), and nothing benefits on the Day of Resurrection except a sound heart. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ لَا يَنْفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart) [Qarai 26:88-89].

His saying, the Exalted: فَالْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ وَلَا مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (So today no ransom shall be taken from you or from the faithless...) [Qarai 57:15]. This is the completion of the speech of the believing men and women, addressing the hypocrites, men and women, and adding to them the disbelievers—those who were open in their disbelief—that they are hostages to their deeds, as the Exalted said: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ (Every soul is a hostage to what it has earned) [Qarai 74:38]. No ransom (فدية) shall be taken from them by which they might free themselves. Ransom is one of two ways by which one is released from being a hostage; the other is a helper who assists and saves, which they negated by saying: مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ (Your abode is the Fire...) [Qarai 57:15].

So His saying: مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ هِيَ مَوْلاكُمْ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ (Your abode is the Fire: it is your master, and it is an evil destination) [Qarai 57:15] negates any helper who might assist them and save them from the Fire, other than the Fire itself, as indicated by the restriction in His saying: "it is your master" (هي مولاكم). The "master" (المولى) is the helper, and the sentence is intended as mockery.

It is also possible that "master" (المولى) means the one who takes charge of one's affairs. For they used to seek their needs of food, drink, clothing, marriage, and housing from other than Allah, the Glorified, and the reality of that was the Fire. So today, their master is the Fire, and it is what prepares those things for them; thus, their food is from Zaqqum, their drink is from boiling water (Hamim), their clothing is from garments cut out of fire, their companions are the devils, and their abode is the Fire, as Allah, the Glorified, has informed in many verses of His speech.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated that Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in al-Dala'il recorded via Zayd ibn Aslam, from 'Ata' ibn Yasar, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the year of Hudaybiyyah. When we were at 'Usfan, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Soon there will come a people whose deeds will make you look down upon your own deeds.' We asked: 'Who are they, O Messenger of Allah? Are they the Quraysh?' He said: 'No, but they are the people of Yemen; they have the most tender hearts and the softest souls.' We asked: 'Are they better than us, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'If one of them had a mountain of gold and spent it, he would not reach the mudd (a measure) of one of you, nor even half of it. Indeed, this is the distinction between us and the people: لَا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَقَاتَلَ (Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought...)'" [Qarai 57:10].

I say: This meaning has been narrated through several chains with similar wording, and they include the verse. However, there is a difficulty in that the apparent context of the verses suggests they were revealed after the conquest—meaning either Hudaybiyyah or the Conquest of Mecca—so it would not apply to what was before the conquest.

In the same source, it is narrated that 'Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn al-Mundhir recorded from 'Ikrimah, who said: "When this verse was revealed: لَا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَقَاتَلَ (Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest and fought...), Abu al-Dahdah said: 'By Allah, I shall spend today a spending by which I will catch up to those before me, and no one after me shall surpass me in it.' He then said: 'O Allah, everything that Abu al-Dahdah owns, half of it is for Allah,' until he even reached his sandal strap and said: 'And this too.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying: يَوْمَ تَرَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ يَسْعى نُورُهُمْ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ (The day you will see the faithful men and the faithful women, with their light moving swiftly before them and on their right...), he said: "The light will be distributed among the people on the Day of Resurrection according to their faith. It will be distributed to the hypocrite such that his light will be at the big toe of his left foot. He will look at his light and then say to the believers: 'Stay in your places so that I may take a light from your light!' The believers will say to them: 'Go back and look for a light!' and a wall will be set up between them with a door. They will call out to the believers from behind the wall: أَلَمْ نَكُنْ مَعَكُمْ قالُوا بَلى وَلكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنْتُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ (Were we not with you? They will say, ‘Yes! But you tempted yourselves...’)." He said: "By sins." وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ وَارْتَبْتُمْ (and you awaited [misfortune for us] and you were in doubt). He said: "Meaning you doubted and awaited misfortune."

Regarding His saying: فَالْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ (So today no ransom shall be taken from you...), he said: "By Allah, He did not mean the Jews or the Christians by that; He meant none but the people of the Qiblah (أهل القبلة)." Then He said: مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ هِيَ مَوْلاكُمْ (Your abode is the Fire: it is your master). He said: "It is more worthy of you (أولى بكم)."

I say: By "people of the Qiblah," he means the hypocrites among them.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Aban ibn Taghlib, who said: "I heard Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام) say: 'Avoid false hopes (المنى), for they take away the splendor of what you have been granted, cause you to belittle the gifts of Allah (جل وعز) to you, and bring you regrets for what you have imagined for yourselves.'"

Ayahs

۞ أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا نَزَلَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا يَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلُ فَطَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (١٦)
Is it not time yet for those who have faith that their hearts should be humbled for Allah’s remembrance and toward the truth which has come down [to them], and to be not like those who were given the Book before? Time took its toll on them and so their hearts were hardened, and many of them are transgressors. (16)
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (١٧)
Know that Allah revives the earth after its death. We have certainly made the signs clear for you, so that you may exercise your reason. (17)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُصَّدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُصَّدِّقَـٰتِ وَأَقْرَضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمْ وَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ (١٨)
Indeed the charitable men and women and those who lend Allah a good loan—it shall be multiplied for them, and there will be a noble reward for them. (18)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصِّدِّيقُونَ ۖ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءُ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٩)
Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and witnesses with their Lord; they shall have their reward and their light. But as for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell. (19)
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ وَزِينَةٌ وَتَفَاخُرٌۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَتَكَاثُرٌ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ ۖ كَمَثَلِ غَيْثٍ أَعْجَبَ ٱلْكُفَّارَ نَبَاتُهُۥ ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَرَىٰهُ مُصْفَرًّا ثُمَّ يَكُونُ حُطَـٰمًا ۖ وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ ۚ وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ (٢٠)
Know that the life of this world is mere diversion and play, glamour and mutual vainglory among you and rivalry for wealth and children—like rain, whose growth impresses the farmer. Then it withers and you see it turn yellow, then it becomes chaff. Whereas in the Hereafter there is forgiveness from Allah and His approval and a severe punishment. The life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion. (20)
سَابِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا كَعَرْضِ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أُعِدَّتْ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٢١)
Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and a paradise as vast as the heavens and the earth, prepared for those who have faith in Allah and His apostles. That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (21)
مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّبْرَأَهَآ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (٢٢)
No affliction visits the land or yourselves but it is in a Book before We bring it about—that is indeed easy for Allah— (22)
لِّكَيْلَا تَأْسَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَاتَكُمْ وَلَا تَفْرَحُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ (٢٣)
so that you may not grieve for what escapes you, nor boast for what comes your way, and Allah does not like any swaggering braggart. (23)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبُخْلِ ۗ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٢٤)
Such as are [themselves] stingy and bid [other] people to be stingy. And whoever refuses to comply [should know that] indeed Allah is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (24)

thoughtful

(Commentary)

The discourse proceeds in accordance with the objective of the preceding speech, which is to urge and encourage faith in Allah and His Messenger, and spending in the way of Allah. It includes a reproach of the believers for the signs of hard-heartedness appearing among them, and reinforces the encouragement to spend by clarifying the rank of the spenders with Allah, commanding them to race toward forgiveness and Paradise, and censuring the world and its people who are parsimonious and command others to be parsimonious.

The context shifts during these verses to a general address encompassing both the Muslims and the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), after the previous context was exclusive to the Muslims; the clarification of this will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللهِ وَما نَزَلَ مِنَ الْحَقِ "Is it not time yet for those who have faith that their hearts should be humbled for the remembrance of Allah and the truth that has come down [to them]..." (57:16) until the end of the verse. It is said: ana ya’ni inan wa ina’an (أنى يأني إنى وإناء), meaning its time has arrived. The humbling (خشوع) of the heart is its being affected in the presence of grandeur and majesty. What is meant by "the remembrance of Allah" (ذكر الله) is that by which Allah is remembered, and "the truth that has come down" (ما نزل من الحق) is the Quran revealed from Him, the Exalted; "of the truth" (مِنَ الْحَقِ) is an explanation of what was revealed. It is the nature of the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted, for a believer that it should be followed by humility, just as it is the nature of the truth revealed from Him, the Exalted, that it should be followed by humility from one who believes in Allah and His messengers.

It has been said that "the remembrance of Allah" and "the truth that has come down" both refer to the Quran. According to this view, the Quran is mentioned by two of its attributes because each attribute necessitates the humility of the believer; the Quran, by virtue of being the remembrance of Allah, demands humility, just as it demands humility by virtue of being the truth revealed from Him, the Exalted.

The verse contains a reproach for the believers regarding the hardness (القسوة) that had overcome their hearts and their lack of humility toward the remembrance of Allah and the truth revealed from Him. It likens their state to that of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — those to whom a scripture was previously revealed — upon whom time was prolonged and thus their hearts became hardened.

His saying: وَلا يَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَطالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ "...and that they should not be like those who were given the Book before: time was prolonged for them and so their hearts hardened..." (57:16) is conjoined to His saying "should be humbled" (تَخْشَعَ). The meaning is: Is it not time for them that their hearts should be humbled and that they should not be, etc. The term al-amad (الأمد) means time (الزمان). Al-Raghib said: "The difference between al-zaman (الزمان) and al-amad (الأمد) is that al-amad is used in consideration of the end-point (الغاية), while al-zaman is general regarding the beginning and the end. For this reason, some have said that al-mada (المدى) and al-amad are close in meaning." End quote.

He, the Glorified, has pointed out with this speech that their hearts were becoming like the hardened hearts of the People of the Book. When the hard heart (القلب القاسي) loses humility and the capacity to be affected by the truth, it may depart from the attire of servitude (زي العبودية), failing to be moved by prohibitions and committing sin and transgression (الفسوق). Therefore, He followed His saying "and so their hearts hardened" with His saying: وَكَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فاسِقُونَ "...and many of them are transgressors." (57:16).

His saying, the Exalted: اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها "Know that Allah revives the earth after its death..." (57:17) until the end of the verse. Following the reproach of the believers for the hardness of their hearts with this parable serves to strengthen their hope and encourage them toward humility.

It is possible that this is a completion of the previous reproach, serving as a reminder that Allah does not leave this religion in such a state; rather, whenever hearts harden and are deprived of humility toward Allah's command, He brings forth hearts that are alive and humble before Him, through which He is worshipped as He desires.

Thus, the verse would be similar in meaning to His saying: ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ تُدْعَوْنَ لِتُنْفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يَبْخَلُ وَمَنْ يَبْخَلْ فَإِنَّما يَبْخَلُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَاللهُ الْغَنِيُّ وَأَنْتُمُ الْفُقَراءُ وَإِنْ تَتَوَلَّوْا يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْماً غَيْرَكُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَكُونُوا أَمْثالَكُمْ "Ah! You are the ones who are invited to spend in the way of Allah; yet among you are those who are stingy; and whoever is stingy is stingy only to himself. Allah is the All-sufficient, and you are the needy. And if you turn away, He will replace you with another people, and they will not be like you." (Surah Muhammad: 38).

For this reason, He concluded the verse by saying: قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ "We have certainly made the signs clear for you, so that you may exercise your reason." (57:17).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْمُصَّدِّقِينَ وَالْمُصَّدِّقاتِ وَأَقْرَضُوا اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً يُضاعَفُ لَهُمْ وَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ "Indeed the charitable men and women and those who lend Allah a good loan—it shall be multiplied for them, and there is a noble reward for them." (57:18). This is a repetition of the discussion regarding multiplication and the noble reward (الأجر الكريم) to encourage spending in the way of Allah. To those who lend Allah a good loan, He added "the charitable men and women" (المصدقون والمصدقات).

The terms al-mussaddiqun and al-mussaddiqat (المصدقون والمصدقات) — with a shadda on the sad and dal — mean those who give charity (المتصدقون والمتصدقات). His saying "and those who lend Allah" (وَأَقْرَضُوا اللهَ) is conjoined to the noun governed by the definite article in "the charitable" (الْمُصَّدِّقِينَ). The meaning is: Indeed, those who give charity and those who lend Allah a good loan shall have what they gave multiplied for them, and they shall have a noble reward.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَالشُّهَداءُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ "Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord..." (57:19) etc. He did not say "faith in Allah and His Messenger" as He said at the beginning of the Surah: "Believe in Allah and His Messenger and spend," and as He said at its end: "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and have faith in His Messenger." This is because when He mentioned the People of the Book in the previous verse by saying "and that they should not be like those who were given the Book before," He shifted from the previous context to a general context that includes both the Muslims and the People of the Book together, as He says later: "Certainly We sent Our apostles with manifest proofs." As for the two verses mentioned at the beginning and end of the Surah, the address in them is specifically for the believers of this Ummah; hence, the Messenger is mentioned in the singular.

What is meant by "faith in Allah and His messengers" is pure faith (الإيمان) — the firm conviction that by its nature does not depart from obedience and following — as previously indicated in His saying: "Believe in Allah and His Messenger." What is meant by His saying "it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses" is their being joined to the ranks of the truthful (الصديقون) and the witnesses (الشهداء), as evidenced by His saying "with their Lord" and His saying "they shall have their reward and their light." Thus, they are joined to these two groups and treated as the truthful and the witnesses, being given the like of their reward and their light.

It appears that what is meant by "the truthful" (الصديقون) and "the witnesses" (الشهداء) are those mentioned in His saying: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the witnesses and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!" (4:69). It has already been mentioned in the commentary of that verse that "the truthful" (الصديقون) are those whose truthfulness permeates their speech and action, so they do what they say and say what they do. "The witnesses" (الشهداء) are the witnesses over deeds on the Day of Resurrection, not "martyrs" in the sense of those killed in the way of Allah.

So, these who believe in Allah and His messengers are joined to the truthful and the witnesses, placed in their station with Allah — that is, by His decree — they shall have their reward and their light.

In His saying: لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ "...they shall have their reward and their light" (57:19), the pronoun "they" (لَهُمْ) refers to those who believe, while the pronoun in "their reward and their light" (أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ) refers to the truthful and the witnesses. That is, those who believe shall have a reward of the type of reward given to the truthful and the witnesses, and a light of the type of their light. This is the meaning intended by those who said the meaning is: they have a reward like their reward and a light like their light.

It has been suggested that the verse is intended to state that they are truthful and witnesses in reality, without any sense of joining or being placed in a station; they are indeed them, and thus have their reward and light. However, the context perhaps does not support this.

It has also been suggested that His saying "and the witnesses" (وَالشُّهَداءُ) is not conjoined to His saying "the truthful" (الصِّدِّيقُونَ), but is rather a new sentence (استئناف). Thus, "the witnesses" is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is "with their Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ), and its other predicate is "they shall have their reward" (لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ). It is said: "Those who have faith in Allah and His messengers—it is they who are the truthful," and the speech is complete. Then it begins anew: "And the witnesses are with their Lord," just as it is said: "rather they are alive with their Lord" (3:169). In this view, "the witnesses" refers to those killed in the way of Allah. Then the speech is completed with His saying: "they shall have their reward and their light."

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ "As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell." (57:19). That is, they shall not depart from it, remaining therein forever.

In this verse, He, the Glorified, has addressed the state of those joined to the truthful and the witnesses — who are the best of people and certainly saved — and the disbelievers who deny His signs — who are the worst of people and certainly perished. There remains a group between the two: the believers who commit sins and offenses, according to their various levels of rebellion against Allah and His Messenger. This is the custom of the Quran in many instances when addressing the state of people on the Day of Resurrection.

This is done to stimulate the faculties of fear (الخوف) and hope (الرجاء) in that intermediate group between the best and the worst, so they may incline toward felicity and choose salvation over destruction.

For this reason, He followed the verse with a censure of the life of this world (الحياة الدنيا), to which those who refuse to spend in the way of Allah are attached. Then He followed it with an invitation to race toward forgiveness and Paradise, and then by pointing out that whatever affliction (مصيبة) befalls them in their wealth or themselves is written in a prior Book and a preceding decree (قضاء). Therefore, they should not fear poverty when spending in the way of Allah, leading them to be stingy and withhold, nor should they fear death in Jihad in the way of Allah, leading them to stay behind and remain idle.

His saying, the Exalted: اعْلَمُوا أَنَّمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ وَزِينَةٌ وَتَفاخُرٌ بَيْنَكُمْ وَتَكاثُرٌ فِي الْأَمْوالِ وَالْأَوْلادِ "Know that the life of this world is but play and diversion, and adornment and boasting among you, and vying in wealth and children..." (57:20) etc. Play (اللعب) is an organized action for an imaginary purpose, like the play of children. Diversion (اللهو) is that which occupies a person away from what is important to him. Adornment (الزينة) is a type of construction, and it may refer to that which one uses to beautify oneself; it is the joining of something desirable to another thing so that the latter becomes desirable through the beauty it has acquired. Boasting (التفاخر) is prideful display of lineage and status. Vying (التكاثر) is the competition in wealth and children.

The life of this world is a fleeting commodity (عرض زائل) and a false mirage (سراب باطل), never free from these five mentioned characteristics: play, diversion, adornment, boasting, and vying. These are the things to which the desire of the human soul attaches, either to some or all of them. They are illusory matters and fleeting accidents that do not remain with the human being; not one of them brings the human being any spiritual perfection (كمال نفسي) or true good (خير حقيقي).

It is narrated from our teacher al-Baha’i (may Allah have mercy on him) that these five characteristics mentioned in the verse are ordered according to the years of a person's life and the stages of his existence. First, he is obsessed with play while a child or adolescent. Then, when he reaches maturity and his frame strengthens, he becomes attached to diversion and amusements. Then, when he reaches full strength, he occupies himself with adornment in the form of fine clothing, elegant mounts, and lofty dwellings, becoming infatuated with grace and beauty. Then, when he reaches middle age, he takes to boasting of status and lineage. Finally, when he becomes old, he strives in vying for wealth and children.

His saying: كَمَثَلِ غَيْثٍ أَعْجَبَ الْكُفَّارَ نَباتُهُ ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَراهُ مُصْفَرًّا ثُمَّ يَكُونُ حُطاماً "...like the example of a rain whose growth impresses the tillers; then it withers and you see it turn yellow, then it becomes chaff." (57:20). This is a parable for the adornment of the life of this world, to which a person attaches himself out of delusion, only for it to be stripped away shortly thereafter.

Al-ghayth (الغيث) is rain. Al-kuffar (الكفار) is the plural of kafir in the sense of a tiller (الحارث). Yahiju (يهيج) is from al-hayajan, which is movement. Al-hutam (الحطام) is dry, broken-up vegetation.

The meaning is: The likeness of the life of this world in its impressive splendor and then its disappearance is like rain whose resulting vegetation impresses the tillers; then it moves toward the limit of its growth, then you see it turned yellow in color, then it becomes broken-up chaff — disintegrated and scattered by the winds.

His saying: وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْوانٌ "...while in the Hereafter there is a severe punishment and forgiveness from Allah and [His] pleasure..." (57:20). Forgiveness (المغفرة) precedes pleasure (الرضوان) to purify the locus so that pleasure may reside therein. Describing forgiveness as being "from Allah" while not doing so for the punishment is not without an intimation that what is intended by primary purpose is forgiveness. As for punishment, it is not intended for its own sake; rather, the human being causes it for himself by departing from the attire of servitude, as has been said.

His saying: وَمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا مَتاعُ الْغُرُورِ "...and the life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion." (57:20). That is, it is a commodity whose enjoyment is itself the delusion by it. This applies to the one who is attached and deluded by it.
